headline: Google ran out of cloud
subtitle: Big Tech spent $130B on AI in Q1 · Google hits capacity limits · Custom silicon emerges as competitive moat
intro: On a single Wednesday, Microsoft, Google, Meta, and Amazon all reported earnings showing AI demand far outpacing infrastructure build capacity. The race to deploy custom chips and secure enterprise workloads is becoming an arms race with trillion-dollar implications.

bullets.text: Hyperscalers spent $130B on AI capex in Q1 alone, nearly double year-ago
bullets.summary: Microsoft's AI business hit a $37B annual run rate with 20M paid M365 Copilot seats. Google Cloud grew 63% with AI products up 800% YoY but faced capacity constraints. AWS grew 28% with custom chips passing $20B run rate. Meta raised 2026 capex guidance to $125-145B.

bullets.text: Run tasks through two models and have them judge each other's work
bullets.summary: Microsoft enabled M365 Copilot to route between GPT and Claude automatically. Different models make different mistakes, so cross-checking outputs catches blind spots neither catches alone. A real example showed DeepSeek V4 fixing memory leaks Claude Opus had missed.

bullets.text: Cursor launches TypeScript SDK for building custom programmatic agents
bullets.summary: Cursor released a TypeScript SDK allowing developers to build custom coding agents with repo context, MCP tools, and automated PR workflows using the same infrastructure as Cursor itself.

bullets.text: House committees probe Airbnb and Cursor's parent over Chinese AI use
bullets.summary: Lawmakers opened investigations citing national security concerns about data sharing with PRC-linked AI labs like Moonshot's Kimi and Alibaba's Qwen.

bullets.text: Google's Flow Music generates songs and music videos from text prompts
bullets.summary: Google's Lyria 3-powered music platform lets users create garage rock songs and music videos with conversational edits, demonstrating AI as a creative amplifier rather than replacement.

main_story.title: Big Tech's $130B AI spending spree reveals infrastructure bottleneck
main_story.body: Microsoft, Google, Meta, and Amazon reported Q1 earnings on the same day, each revealing massive spending on AI infrastructure. Google Cloud grew 63% with AI products surging 800% year-over-year, but CEO Sundar Pichai admitted the cloud division couldn't build fast enough to meet demand. AWS's custom chip business passed $20B annual run rate with OpenAI and Anthropic committing significant capacity to Amazon's Trainium processors. Combined capex across the four hyperscalers reached roughly $130B in a single quarter, nearly double the prior year.
main_story.why: Over a trillion dollars in signed enterprise commitments now sit undelivered across the hyperscalers' backlogs, signaling unprecedented demand for AI infrastructure. The shift from renting commodity hardware to deploying custom silicon has fundamentally changed competitive advantage in cloud computing.
main_story.take: Cloud growth rates no longer indicate who's winning—concrete poured and custom silicon do. Amazon may be quietly winning the chip war by becoming the supplier of choice for OpenAI and Anthropic, inverting its historical business model. The real risk emerges if capex spending continues climbing without matching revenue gains, as Amazon's free cash flow has already collapsed 95% due to these investments.

around_the_horn.text: Claude Mythos Preview discovered 271 zero-day vulnerabilities in Firefox 150
Mayo Clinic AI spots pancreatic cancer on routine CT scans 475 days early
Anthropic releases Introspection Adapters for LLM self-reporting misalignment
Mistral releases Medium 3.5 model with 256k context and remote coding agents
Seven families sue OpenAI over alleged ChatGPT negligence in mass shooting
Gemini app generates Google Docs, Sheets, Slides directly from chat prompts

previous.headline: Anthropic Hits $1T, Passes OpenAI in Valuation Race
previous.subtitle: Anthropic's $1T milestone · Creative workflow integrations · OpenAI revenue miss and legal troubles
previous.intro: Anthropic crossed a $1 trillion valuation yesterday, becoming the world's most valuable AI company, while OpenAI stumbled with missed revenue projections and faced new legal challenges. On the same day, Anthropic announced deep integrations with creative tools like Adobe, Blender, and Autodesk, signaling a shift in competitive strategy from raw model performance to workflow depth.

bullets.summary: Anthropic officially became the most valuable AI company after crossing a $1 trillion valuation. On the same day, OpenAI missed revenue projections, faced Elon Musk's lawsuit alleging stolen funds, and watched Oracle and chip stocks decline in response.

bullets.text: Anthropic launches Claude integrations for creative tools
bullets.summary: Anthropic released Claude for Creative Work with native connectors for Blender, Adobe Creative Cloud, Autodesk Fusion, Ableton, Splice, SketchUp, Resolume, and Canva. The integrations enable Claude to debug 3D scenes, batch-edit assets, and manipulate professional creative workflows directly.

bullets.text: Adobe releases official Claude connector for 50+ tools
bullets.summary: Adobe simultaneously launched its own Claude connector providing live access to 50+ professional tools across Photoshop, Premiere, Firefly, and InDesign. This enables Claude to orchestrate multi-app creative workflows without manual handoffs between applications.

bullets.text: Both labs should build hardware to survive cost pressures
bullets.summary: The newsletter argues that cloud token business economics are unsustainable, with AI compute costs now exceeding employee costs at most companies. OpenAI is reportedly building a phone, while Anthropic's creative plays position it well for eventual hardware that could run local agents, similar to Apple and Microsoft's strategies.

bullets.text: AI buildout costs reveal profitability questions
bullets.summary: Multiple analyses examine whether AI productivity gains will justify massive infrastructure investments. Critics point to token costs running $8-$13 per dollar of revenue, overbuilt data centers, and persistent lack of measurable productivity ROI from enterprise deployments.

main_story.title: The Race Has Shifted From Model Smarts to Workflow Integration
main_story.body: The competitive landscape between Anthropic and OpenAI has fundamentally transformed. Rather than chasing benchmark wins every six weeks with marginally improved models, the real battle is now about embedding AI into the tools people already use daily. Anthropic's single-day announcements—crossing $1 trillion in valuation while launching integrations across Adobe, Blender, and other professional suites—demonstrate this new strategic imperative. Meanwhile, OpenAI faced revenue misses and legal setbacks, suggesting the chat interface era is saturated and the moat has shifted to workflow depth.
main_story.why: Control over where users interact with AI represents enormous leverage. The company that embeds its agent deepest into existing applications and eventually the devices themselves (phones, computers, glasses) will become the default AI layer across all software. This explains why the newsletter argues both labs must eventually manufacture hardware to survive the economics of subsidizing cloud compute indefinitely.
main_story.take: The $1T valuation is a lagging indicator of this shift; the real competition will be determined by who can best position themselves to sell the device your local AI agent runs on. Anthropic's creative integrations are workflow-light and compute-efficient, a natural foundation for agent software that lives on your Mac. OpenAI's reported phone project and Anthropic's hardware path suggest both labs understand they cannot remain pure software companies.

around_the_horn.text: Anthropic now requires photo ID and selfie verification for some Claude users to enforce policies.
Microsoft released World-R1, an RL framework injecting 3D physical constraints into text-to-video models.
Freepik officially rebranded to Magnific as a unified professional creative platform.
EU ordered Google to open Android's AI ecosystem to allow competing assistants beyond Gemini.
Google and Pentagon signed classified deal authorizing DoD to use Google's AI for lawful government purposes.
Planned gas-powered AI data centers could emit 129+ million tons of greenhouse gases annually.
